items.q: What is lag time on a storm hydrograph?
items.correct: The time between peak rainfall and peak river discharge — affected by drainage basin shape, land use, soil saturation and vegetation.
items.misconception: Lag time is how long the storm itself lasts.
items.random_wrong: Lag time is the delay between thunder and lightning.
items.why: Students misread the graph. Lag time is the gap between maximum rain and maximum river flow — it tells you how quickly the basin responds, not the duration of rainfall.

items.q: In a tropical rainforest nutrient cycle, where is most of the nutrient stored?
items.correct: In the biomass (living plants). Litter is rapidly decomposed by warm humid conditions, and soil is leached — so cycling between biomass and litter dominates.
items.misconception: Most nutrients in a rainforest are stored in the soil — that's why plants grow so well.
items.random_wrong: Most rainforest nutrients are stored in animals.
items.why: Rainforest soils are notoriously thin and infertile. The Gersmehl model shows biomass as the dominant store — which is why slash-and-burn agriculture collapses quickly when biomass is removed.

items.q: What happens during an El Nino event?
items.correct: Trade winds weaken, warm water that normally pools in the western Pacific spreads east, disrupting weather patterns globally — drought in Australia, floods in South America.
items.misconception: El Nino is when the ocean gets colder near Peru.
items.random_wrong: El Nino is the name of a specific hurricane that hit Mexico.
items.why: El Nino brings WARM water to Peru — the cold-water state is La Nina. Students mix up the two phases; the name 'the Christ child' refers to warm currents at Christmas.
The answer examiners want is: Tundra. “Rainforest” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Near the Equator. “Near the poles” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Roughly what's the average temperature in a tropical rainforest (°C)?
items.why: The real figure is 28. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
